OpenCalphad Study

A practical exploration of thermodynamic modelling using computational tools.

Understanding materials through simulation, not just theory.

Explore Project →
Fundamentals

Building the foundation

Core concepts behind OpenCalphad, from the CALPHAD method to Gibbs energy minimisation and data visualisation with Gnuplot.

01 Introduction to OpenCalphad

OpenCalphad (OC) is a free, open-source computational thermodynamics software for calculating phase equilibria and constructing phase diagrams in multicomponent alloy systems. It lets materials scientists predict which phases are stable under any given temperature, pressure, and composition, removing the need for costly trial-and-error experiments.

The CALPHAD Method

OpenCalphad is built on the CALPHAD method (CALculation of PHAse Diagrams), the internationally accepted framework for computational thermodynamics.

CALPHAD works by combining experimental data with thermodynamic models to build mathematical descriptions of the Gibbs energy for every phase in a system. Once these descriptions exist in a database, phase equilibria can be calculated for any composition or temperature, including complex multicomponent alloys that could never be fully mapped by experiment alone.

The key insight is extrapolation: a CALPHAD database built from well-studied binary and ternary systems can reliably predict behaviour in 5-, 6-, or even 10-component industrial alloys.

Input
  • Elements (Fe, C, Cr…)
  • Composition
  • Temperature / Pressure
  • Database (.TDB)
→
Process
  • Gibbs energy models
  • Free energy minimisation
  • Equilibrium convergence
→
Output
  • Stable phases
  • Phase fractions
  • Phase diagrams

How OpenCalphad Works

OC determines equilibrium by finding the combination of phases with the lowest total Gibbs free energy. It reads Gibbs energy expressions for every phase from a .TDB thermodynamic database file, then uses a numerical minimiser to find the stable phase assemblage. The database is the model, and without it no calculation is possible.

Real-world Applications

  • Steel and iron alloy design (phase transformations, carbide stability)
  • Ni-superalloy and Al-alloy development
  • Solidification and casting simulation
  • Heat treatment optimisation
  • Corrosion, slag, and high-temperature chemistry

Limitations

  • Predicts equilibrium only, with no kinetic or diffusion information directly
  • Accuracy is entirely dependent on database quality
  • The CAE (graphical) interface makes basic tasks easier, but advanced usage still requires learning command-line inputs, which can take time

02 Introduction to Gnuplot

Gnuplot is a free, command-line plotting program widely used in scientific computing. OpenCalphad generates numerical output, such as tables of temperatures, compositions and phase fractions. Gnuplot takes that data and turns it into phase diagrams, property diagrams and other graphs, making results easier to interpret and share.

OpenCalphad CAE calls Gnuplot automatically whenever a plot is generated, as long as it is installed and linked correctly. Users can customise axis labels, line styles and legends through OC CAE's "Modified Gnuplot" output option. In short, Gnuplot is the visualisation layer that brings OpenCalphad's results to life.

Key point: Gnuplot is a separate program that must be installed alongside OC CAE. The installation guide below walks through this process step by step.

Setup

OpenCalphad Installation Guide

Complete step-by-step instructions for installing OpenCalphad CAE and linking it with Gnuplot on Windows, with real screenshots from the setup process.

A

Installing OpenCalphad CAE

Download and install the OC_CAE application on Windows.

📦 Download OpenCalphad CAE

Download OC_CAE_Setup_ver007.exe or above from the official Dropbox folder containing the installer along with sample databases and documentation.

1

Handle the Windows SmartScreen warning

Double-click the installer. Windows Defender SmartScreen will show a blue warning. Click "More info" then "Run anyway" to proceed.

Step 1: Click Run Anyway

2

Accept the Licence Agreement

The OpenCalphad CAE Setup wizard opens. Read the EULA, select "I accept the agreement", then click Next.

Step 2: Accept the licence agreement

3

Choose the installation folder

The default destination is C:\OpenCalphad_CAE_0_0_7. Keep this default to avoid path-related issues later. Click Next.

Step 3: Keep default path and click Next

4

Review settings and click Install

The "Ready to Install" screen summarises your chosen destination and shortcuts. Confirm these are correct, then click Install.

Step 4: Click Install

5

Finish and launch OC CAE

When setup completes, the "Completing the OpenCalphad_CAE Setup Wizard" screen appears. Ensure "Launch OpenCalphad_CAE" is ticked, then click Finish. The application opens immediately.

Step 5: Click Finish to launch OC CAE

G

Installing Gnuplot

Link Gnuplot with OpenCalphad CAE for plot generation.

1

Open the Tools tab and select Install Gnuplot

In OpenCalphad CAE, go to the Tools menu and click Install Gnuplot.

Gnuplot Step 1: Open the Install Gnuplot dialog

2

Download Gnuplot

The Install Gnuplot dialog opens. Click Install Gnuplot to download the installer from SourceForge.

Gnuplot Step 2: Click Install Gnuplot

3

Select Language

A language selection prompt appears. Choose English and click OK.

Gnuplot Step 3: Select Language

4

Accept the Gnuplot licence and click Next

Read the licence, select "I accept the agreement", then click Next.

Gnuplot Step 4: Accept agreement and click Next

5

Read information and click Next

An information screen appears with Gnuplot details. Click Next to continue.

Gnuplot Step 5: Click Next

6

Keep default installation path

The default location is C:\Program Files (x86)\gnuplot. Keep it to avoid path issues later. Click Next.

Gnuplot Step 6: Keep default path

7

Select components and click Next

Keep the default "Full installation" selected. Click Next.

Gnuplot Step 7: Select components

8

Select Start Menu folder and click Next

Keep the default Start Menu folder for gnuplot. Click Next.

Gnuplot Step 8: Select Start Menu folder

9

Select default terminal

Under "Select gnuplot's default terminal", choose "Don't change my GNUTERM environment variable" and click Next.

Gnuplot Step 9: Select Don't change my GNUTERM

10

Click Install

The "Ready to Install" screen appears. Click Install to begin installation.

Gnuplot Step 10: Click Install

11

Installation in progress

Gnuplot installs its components. Wait for the process to complete.

Gnuplot Step 11: Installation in progress

12

Click Finish

The "Completing the gnuplot Setup Wizard" screen appears. Click Finish.

Gnuplot Step 12: Click Finish

13

Verify and configure Gnuplot

Back in OC CAE, click Verify and config Gnuplot. A Gnuplot window opens with a sample plot.

Gnuplot Step 13: Verify configuration

14

Gnuplot window appears

A Gnuplot plot window should appear, showing that the link between OC CAE and Gnuplot is working correctly.

Gnuplot Step 14: Gnuplot window appears

It passed the verification!

✅

Verification passed!

OC CAE confirms the installation is successful. You are now ready to run thermodynamic calculations and generate plots.

Examples

Step-by-step case studies

Worked examples demonstrating OpenCalphad CAE across single equilibrium, property diagrams, phase diagrams and solidification calculations.

Case 1-1

Single Equilibrium Calculation: Fe–C–Cr

Objective
To study which phases are stable and their fractions for an Fe–0.1 wt% C–1 wt% Cr alloy at 1000 K.
Plot
📊 Insert plot for Case 1-1 (Single Equilibrium: Fe-C-Cr pie chart)
Conclusions
  • At 1000 K, the alloy is dominated by BCC-A2 (ferrite), constituting nearly the entire structure (~98.95%).
  • A small amount of alloy carbide M7C3 (~0.87%) is also present, along with a very minor fraction of cementite (~0.17%).
  • Even a small addition of chromium (~1%) promotes the formation of complex carbides rather than maintaining a simple solid solution.
Case 2-1

Property Diagram Calculation: Fe–C

Objective
To understand how phase fractions change with temperature for steels with different carbon contents (0.1 wt% C and 1 wt% C).
Plots
📊 Insert plot (a) for Case 2-1 — Fe-1%C: Mole fraction of phase vs Temperature
📊 Insert plot (b) for Case 2-1 — Fe-0.1%C: Temperature vs Composition
Conclusions
  • The plots clearly show how phases evolve during cooling: starting from liquid, forming austenite (FCC), and finally ferrite (BCC).
  • With higher carbon content, the liquid phase remains stable over a wider temperature range and the austenite region becomes larger.
  • The diagram helps identify temperatures at which carbon leaves the solid solution and forms phases like graphite or cementite.
Case 3-1

Phase Diagram Calculation: Fe–C

Objective
To generate a temperature vs composition (T–X) diagram for the Fe–C binary system.
Plots
📊 Insert plot (a) for Case 3-1 — Fe-C: Mole fraction vs Temperature (W(C)=0.001)
📊 Insert plot (b) for Case 3-1 — Fe-C: Temperature vs Composition (w% C)
Conclusions
  • The diagram correctly shows important invariant reactions: the eutectic point (~1420 K at 4.3% C) and the eutectoid point (~1000 K at 0.76% C).
  • It defines the stability regions for liquid, austenite (FCC), and ferrite (BCC) — essential for understanding steels and cast irons.
  • The difference in carbon solubility between FCC and BCC structures is clearly visible, forming the basis for heat treatment processes.
Case 3-3

Ternary Phase Diagram: Fe–Cr–Ni

Objective
To study phase stability at constant temperatures (1000 K and 1200 K) and understand how chromium and nickel influence the phase diagram.
Plots
📊 Insert plot (a) for Case 3-3 — Fe-Cr-Ni ternary isothermal section at T=1000 K
📊 Insert plot (b) for Case 3-3 — Fe-Cr-Ni ternary isothermal section at T=1200 K
📊 Insert plot (c) for Case 3-3 — Fe-Cr-Ni ternary section showing Fe-axis projection at T=1200 K
Conclusions
  • Nickel stabilises austenite (FCC) while chromium stabilises ferrite (BCC); their balance controls the resulting phase.
  • The sigma (σ) phase region is clearly identified — a brittle intermetallic that is generally undesirable in engineering applications.
  • Tie-lines and phase regions reveal how elements distribute between phases, which is crucial for designing duplex stainless steels.
Case 3-5

Ternary Phase Diagram: Fe–C–Cr

Objective
To analyse phase stability in the Fe–Cr–C system at a constant temperature of 1273 K (≈ 1000 °C).
Plot
📊 Insert plot for Case 3-5 — Fe-C-Cr: Temperature vs W%(C) at W(CR)=0.13
Conclusions
  • At 1273 K the austenite (FCC) region is large, indicating chromium and carbon are well dissolved in the matrix.
  • The diagram shows conditions under which alloy carbides like M7C3 and M23C6 begin to form, depending on carbon and chromium content.
  • Phase boundaries clearly separate single-phase and multi-phase regions, enabling prediction of the equilibrium microstructure.
  • Tie-lines indicate how carbon and chromium distribute between the matrix and carbide phases at equilibrium.
